questions.question: Chart junk refers to:
options: Visual elements that add ink without adding information -- gridlines, 3D effects, decorations
questions.explanation: Edward Tufte coined 'chart junk'. Every pixel should carry information. Remove: 3D effects, heavy borders, unnecessary gridlines, decorative icons.

questions.question: Pre-attentive attributes are visual properties that:
options: The human eye detects automatically before conscious attention -- colour, size, shape, position
questions.explanation: Pre-attentive: colour, position, size, orientation. Using colour to highlight the key bar means viewers see it instantly without reading everything.

questions.question: 'Put the insight in the title, not just the variable name' means:
options: Instead of 'Monthly Revenue' write 'Revenue grew 64% in H1 -- fastest growth since 2020'
questions.explanation: 'Monthly Revenue' is a label. 'Revenue peaked in June -- H1 target exceeded' is an insight. The reader knows what to take away.

questions.question: When should you use a pie chart?
options: Only when: parts sum to 100%, there are fewer than 5 slices, and differences are visually obvious
questions.explanation: Humans are poor at comparing angles/areas. Bar charts almost always communicate part-of-whole better than pies.

questions.question: The main problem with 3D bar charts is:
options: They distort proportions -- depth creates visual illusions that misrepresent the actual values
questions.explanation: 3D perspective makes bars in the back appear shorter than bars in front even when equal. Always use 2D.

questions.question: Direct labelling vs legend in a line chart:
options: Direct labels on each line (right-hand side) are faster to read -- readers don't scan back and forth to a legend
questions.explanation: For multiple lines, label each line directly at the end. Legends force eye movement: read legend, find colour, find line, repeat.

questions.question: A diverging colour scale (red-white-green) is appropriate for:
options: Data with a meaningful midpoint -- e.g. profit/loss where 0 is neutral, positive=green, negative=red
questions.explanation: Diverging scales: red=bad, green=good, white=neutral. Don't use for sequential data (temperatures, counts) where there's no neutral midpoint.

questions.question: When a chart needs three or more colours to distinguish categories, you should consider:
options: Splitting into multiple focused charts or using a single metric that captures what matters
questions.explanation: More than 3-4 colours are hard to distinguish. Simplify or split. Use colour for ONE thing -- the most important distinction.

questions.question: The data-ink ratio principle (Tufte) says:
options: Use as much colour as possible
Maximise the proportion of ink dedicated to actual data -- minimise decorative, non-data ink
Always use the full chart area
questions.explanation: Every element should earn its place. Heavy borders, background fill, shadow effects, 3D perspectives all violate this principle.

questions.question: 'Tell one story per chart' means:
options: Each chart should have one clear message -- multiple insights should be split into multiple charts
questions.explanation: Trying to show everything in one chart creates confusion. A series of focused charts tells a clearer story than one cluttered chart.
